name: testing
context: Run and write automated tests — unit, integration, end-to-end — across JavaScript, TypeScript, and Python projects
info: The testing ecosystem covers the full spectrum of automated testing for software projects. It spans unit testing frameworks (Jest, Vitest, pytest), end-to-end browser testing (Playwright), cross-cutting testing patterns (Arrange-Act-Assert, given-when-then, test factories, test doubles), mocking strategies for isolating units under test, and CI integration for running tests in automated pipelines. Agents use this ecosystem to write new tests, run existing suites, debug failures, configure test infrastructure, and integrate testing into deployment workflows. Each skill node provides framework-specific knowledge with executable scripts, while context nodes teach transferable concepts that apply across frameworks.
body:
# Testing Ecosystem

Automated testing is the foundation of reliable software delivery. This ecosystem organizes testing knowledge into framework-specific skills and cross-cutting concepts.

## Framework Skills

- **[[testing/jest]]** — The standard test runner for JavaScript and TypeScript projects using CommonJS or legacy setups. Mature ecosystem with built-in mocking, snapshots, and code coverage.
- **[[testing/vitest]]** — Modern Vite-native test framework for TypeScript and ESM-first projects. API-compatible with Jest but faster, with native ESM and TypeScript support.
- **[[testing/pytest]]** — The de facto Python testing framework. Fixture-based dependency injection, parametrize for data-driven tests, rich plugin ecosystem.
- **[[testing/playwright]]** — Cross-browser end-to-end testing. Automates Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit with auto-waiting, network interception, and visual comparisons.

## Cross-Cutting Concepts

- **[[testing/patterns]]** — Structural testing patterns: Arrange-Act-Assert, Given-When-Then, test factories, builder patterns, property-based testing.
- **[[testing/mocking]]** — Mocking strategies across frameworks: stubs, spies, fakes, dependency injection, when to mock vs. when to use real implementations.
- **[[testing/ci]]** — Running tests in CI/CD pipelines: GitHub Actions, parallelization, test splitting, flaky test management, coverage reporting.

## Choosing a Framework

| Need | Framework | Why |
|------|-----------|-----|
| JS/TS unit tests (CJS/legacy) | [[testing/jest]] | Mature, huge ecosystem, built-in mocking |
| JS/TS unit tests (ESM/Vite) | [[testing/vitest]] | Native ESM, fast HMR, Jest-compatible API |
| Python unit/integration tests | [[testing/pytest]] | Fixtures, parametrize, plugin ecosystem |
| Browser E2E tests | [[testing/playwright]] | Cross-browser, auto-wait, network mocking |
| API integration tests | [[testing/jest]] or [[testing/vitest]] + supertest | HTTP assertions against running servers |
| Visual regression | [[testing/playwright]] | Screenshot comparison built in |

sections.title: Choosing the right framework
sections.body: For JavaScript/TypeScript projects: use [[testing/vitest]] for new projects (ESM-first, Vite-native, fast) or [[testing/jest]] for existing projects already using Jest or CommonJS. For Python: [[testing/pytest]] is the standard — it runs unittest.TestCase too. For browser testing: [[testing/playwright]] handles all major browsers with a single API. Don't mix unit test frameworks in one project — pick one and stick with it.

sections.title: The test pyramid
sections.body: The test pyramid guides investment: many fast unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, fewest E2E tests at the top. Unit tests (Jest/Vitest/pytest) are fast and cheap — test logic in isolation. Integration tests verify component interactions — database queries, API endpoints, service boundaries. E2E tests (Playwright) verify full user flows through the real UI. Inverting the pyramid (heavy E2E, few unit tests) leads to slow, flaky suites. See [[testing/patterns]] for structural patterns within each layer.

sections.title: Test-driven development
sections.body: TDD follows the red-green-refactor cycle: write a failing test (red), write the minimum code to pass (green), then refactor while keeping tests green. TDD works well for algorithmic code, business logic, and API contracts. For UI code, write tests after building the component but before shipping. All frameworks in this ecosystem support TDD workflows — see each skill node for framework-specific watch modes and fast feedback loops.

related.note: TypeScript projects use Jest or Vitest for testing
Python projects use pytest for testing

author: dojo-community
